items.subtopic: Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE
items.title: Classification of Headaches: Primary vs. Secondary
items.content: Headaches are classified as primary or secondary based on their origin. Primary headaches, such as migraines, form the disorder itself and can significantly impact quality of life. Secondary headaches result from external disorders and are often less concerning unless they indicate a serious underlying condition.
keyPoints: Primary headaches originate from headache and its associated features themselves.
Secondary headaches are caused by exogenous disorders.
Vigilance is required for recognizing potentially life-threatening secondary headaches.
items.reference: Headache · Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE
items.title: Anatomy and Physiology of Headache: Pain Pathways
items.content: Headaches can result from the stimulation of peripheral nociceptors or inappropriate activation within pain-producing pathways. Key structures involved in primary headaches include the trigeminovascular system, which involves the large intracranial vessels, dura mater, and trigeminal nerve terminals.
keyPoints: Pain occurs when peripheral nociceptors are stimulated by tissue injury or other factors.
Primary headache structures: large intracranial vessels, dura mater, and trigeminal nerve terminals.
The trigeminovascular system is central to primary headaches.
items.reference: Headache · Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE
items.title: Misconceptions about Vascular Changes in Migraine and Cluster Headaches
items.content: Migraines and cluster headaches involve cranial autonomic symptoms due to activation of cranial parasympathetic pathways. These vascular changes are secondary events, not causative, and should not be used to diagnose or predict treatment outcomes for these disorders.
keyPoints: Cranial autonomic symptoms in TACs reflect activation of cranial parasympathetic pathways.
Vascular changes in migraine and cluster headache are secondary, not causative events.
Migraine is a brain disorder, not a 'vascular headache'.
items.reference: Headache · Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE
items.title: Importance of Prompt Evaluation in New-Onset Severe Headache
items.content: Patients presenting with a new and severe headache have a higher probability of having a serious underlying cause compared to those with recurrent headaches. Rapid evaluation is essential to identify potentially life-threatening conditions such as meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or tumors.
keyPoints: New-onset severe headache has a greater likelihood of serious causes than recurrent headaches.
Prompt evaluation and treatment are crucial for patients with recent onset of pain.
Serious causes include meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, epidural/subdural hematoma, glaucoma, tumor, purulent sinusitis.
items.reference: Headache · CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE
items.title: Role of Neurologic Examination and Imaging in Acute Headache Evaluation
items.content: A thorough neurologic examination is critical in the initial evaluation of new-onset severe headache. Patients with abnormal findings or recent onset should undergo CT or MRI imaging to screen for intracranial pathology, as both methods are equally sensitive.
keyPoints: Neurologic examination is essential in evaluating new-onset severe headaches.
CT and MRI are equally sensitive for initial screening of intracranial pathology.
Lumbar puncture may be required unless a benign cause can be established.
items.reference: Headache · CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE

items.title: Diagnosing Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: When to Use LP
items.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) presents with acute, severe headache that peaks in less than 5 minutes and lasts more than 5 minutes. It is often accompanied by a stiff neck but without fever. A normal head CT scan does not rule out SAH if the hemorrhage is small or located below the foramen magnum; thus, lumbar puncture (LP) may be necessary to confirm diagnosis.
keyPoints: Acute headache peaking in <5 min and lasting >5 min with stiff neck suggests subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Normal head CT scan does not exclude SAH if the hemorrhage is small or below foramen magnum.
LP may be required to definitively diagnose subarachnoid hemorrhage.
items.reference: Headache · MENINGITIS | INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE | BRAIN TUMOR

items.title: Characteristics of Headache in Brain Tumor Patients
items.content: Approximately 30% of patients with brain tumors report headache as their chief complaint. The headache is typically nondescript, characterized by intermittent deep, dull aching that may worsen with exertion or positional changes and can be associated with nausea and vomiting. Vomiting preceding headache by weeks is highly indicative of posterior fossa brain tumors.
keyPoints: 30% of patients with brain tumors report headache as their chief complaint.
Headache in brain tumor patients is often a nondescript, intermittent deep, dull ache.
Vomiting preceding headache by weeks suggests posterior fossa brain tumors.
items.reference: Headache · MENINGITIS | INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE | BRAIN TUMOR

items.title: Recognize and Treat Temporal Arteritis Promptly to Prevent Blindness
items.content: Temporal arteritis is a critical condition in the elderly due to its potential to cause rapidly developing blindness. It involves inflammation of extracranial arteries, most notably affecting the ophthalmic artery. Immediate treatment with glucocorticoids can prevent this severe complication.
keyPoints: Annual incidence: 77 per 100,000 individuals aged ≥50
Average age of onset: 70 years
Women account for 65% of cases
About half of untreated patients develop blindness due to ischemic optic neuropathy
Prompt recognition and treatment with glucocorticoids are crucial
items.reference: Headache · TEMPORAL ARTERITIS

items.title: Diagnosing Temporal Arteritis: Clinical Suspicion and Biopsy
items.content: Temporal arteritis presents with headache, jaw claudication, fever, weight loss, and scalp tenderness. Diagnosis is confirmed by temporal artery biopsy when clinical suspicion is high, despite ESR not being always elevated.
keyPoints: Headache often unilateral or bilateral, located temporally in 50% of patients
Scalp tenderness may be marked; pain aggravated by cold exposure
ESR is often elevated but not definitive for diagnosis
Initiate temporal artery biopsy and prednisone treatment (80 mg daily) when suspicion is high
items.reference: Headache · TEMPORAL ARTERITIS

items.subtopic: GLAUCOMA | PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS | CHRONIC DAILY OR NEAR-DAILY HEADACHE | Chronic Daily Headache
items.title: Recognizing Glaucoma as a Secondary Cause of Headache
items.content: Glaucoma can present with prostrating headaches accompanied by nausea and vomiting, often starting with severe eye pain. Physical examination may reveal red eyes and fixed, moderately dilated pupils.
keyPoints: Headache associated with glaucoma includes severe eye pain and nausea/vomiting.
Physical signs include a red eye and a fixed, moderately dilated pupil.
items.reference: Headache · GLAUCOMA | PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS | CHRONIC DAILY OR NEAR-DAILY HEADACHE | Chronic Daily Headache

items.subtopic: GLAUCOMA | PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS | CHRONIC DAILY OR NEAR-DAILY HEADACHE | Chronic Daily Headache
items.title: Management of Chronic Daily Headache (CDH)
items.content: The management of CDH involves diagnosing and treating any secondary headache. For primary headaches, identifying the type guides therapy. Preventive treatments like tricyclics are effective for CDH from migraine or tension-type headaches.
keyPoints: Diagnose and treat secondary headaches in CDH patients first.
Preventive treatment with tricyclics (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) up to 1 mg/kg is useful.
Tricyclics are started at low doses (10–25 mg) and may be given before awakening to avoid sleepiness.
Other medications include topiramate, valproate, propranolol, candesartan, CGRP pathway monoclonal antibodies, and gepants.
items.reference: Headache · GLAUCOMA | PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS | CHRONIC DAILY OR NEAR-DAILY HEADACHE | Chronic Daily Headache

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF MEDICALLY INTRACTABLE DISABLING PRIMARY HEADACHE | MEDICATION-RELATED AND MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE
items.title: Use of Monoclonal Antibodies and Neuromodulation in Chronic Migraine Management
items.content: Monoclonal antibodies targeting CGRP or its receptor, along with gepants (CGRP receptor antagonists), are effective for chronic migraine management. Noninvasive neuromodulatory techniques like transcranial magnetic stimulation and vagal nerve stimulation show promise, especially in cluster headache and related disorders.
keyPoints: Monoclonal antibodies to CGRP or its receptor and gepants are licensed for clinical use in chronic migraine.
Noninvasive neuromodulation includes single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation and vagal nerve stimulation.
Vagal nerve stimulation is particularly promising for cluster headache, chronic paroxysmal hemicrania, and related conditions.
items.reference: Headache · MANAGEMENT OF MEDICALLY INTRACTABLE DISABLING PRIMARY HEADACHE | MEDICATION-RELATED AND MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF MEDICALLY INTRACTABLE DISABLING PRIMARY HEADACHE | MEDICATION-RELATED AND MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE
items.title: Approach to Medication Overuse Headache in Outpatients
items.content: For outpatients with medication overuse headache, reducing and eliminating analgesic use is key. Gradual dose reduction by 10% every 1–2 weeks or immediate cessation can be effective, supported by a medication diary. NSAIDs like naproxen (500 mg bid) may help manage residual pain during this period.
keyPoints: Reduce and eliminate analgesic use to manage medication overuse headache.
Gradual dose reduction: 10% every 1–2 weeks or immediate cessation if no contraindication.
Use a medication diary to track and identify the scope of the problem.
NSAID (e.g., naproxen, 500 mg bid) can help manage residual pain during analgesic reduction.
items.reference: Headache · MANAGEMENT OF MEDICALLY INTRACTABLE DISABLING PRIMARY HEADACHE | MEDICATION-RELATED AND MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF MEDICALLY INTRACTABLE DISABLING PRIMARY HEADACHE | MEDICATION-RELATED AND MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE
items.title: Inpatient Management of Medication Overuse Headache
items.content: Hospitalization may be necessary for patients with medication overuse headache who fail outpatient withdrawal or have complicating medical conditions. Complete withdrawal on the first day, supplemented by antiemetics and fluids, is recommended. IV dihydroergotamine (DHE) administered every 8 hours for 5 days can induce remission, allowing preventive treatment to be established.
keyPoints: Hospitalization may be required for detoxification in medication overuse headache.
Complete withdrawal of medications on the first day if no contraindication exists.
IV dihydroergotamine (DHE) administered every 8 hours for 5 days can induce remission.
Use antiemetics and fluids to manage symptoms; consider serotonin receptor antagonists or neurokinin receptor antagonists with DHE.
items.reference: Headache · MANAGEMENT OF MEDICALLY INTRACTABLE DISABLING PRIMARY HEADACHE | MEDICATION-RELATED AND MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE

items.title: Distinguishing Primary from Secondary NDPH
items.content: New daily persistent headache (NDPH) requires differentiation between primary and secondary causes, with subarachnoid hemorrhage being the most critical secondary cause to exclude.
keyPoints: Secondary causes of NDPH are important and include low CSF volume headache.
Subarachnoid hemorrhage is a serious secondary cause that must be excluded by history or investigation.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Low CSF Volume Headache
items.content: Low CSF volume headache presents as positional, worsening with upright posture and improving upon reclining. It often follows a lumbar puncture or other events causing CSF leak.
keyPoints: Headache is positional, starting when sitting or standing and resolving on reclining.
Post-LP headache usually begins within 48 hours but may be delayed up to 12 days.
Incidence of post-LP headache is between 10-30%.
CSF pressures can range from 0–50 mm CSF, with documented leaks noted even at 140 mm CSF.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 1)

items.title: Imaging and Treatment for Low CSF Volume Headache
items.content: MRI with gadolinium is the initial imaging choice to identify a presumed CSF leak, showing diffuse meningeal enhancement. Initial treatment includes bed rest and IV caffeine.
keyPoints: MRI with gadolinium shows typical diffuse meningeal enhancement for diagnosis.
Initial treatment involves bed rest; IV caffeine (500 mg in 500 mL saline over 2 hours) is effective.
An ECG should be performed before administering IV caffeine to screen for arrhythmia.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 1)

items.title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension and NDPH
items.content: New Daily Persistent Headache (NDPH) can be associated with raised cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) pressure, often presenting as idiopathic intracranial hypertension. This condition is linked to obesity, female gender, and sometimes pregnancy. Diagnosis involves evaluating CSF pressure through lumbar puncture (LP), particularly when symptoms are present, to confirm elevated opening pressures and symptomatic improvement post-CSF removal.
keyPoints: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension associated with obesity, female gender, and pregnancy
Headache history: present on awakening, improves during the day, worsens with recumbency
Transient visual obscurations common
Diagnosis via LP when symptomatic; elevated opening pressure and headache improvement post-CSF removal are diagnostic
Initial treatment: acetazolamide (250–500 mg bid); consider weight loss
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Raised CSF Pressure in NDPH
items.content: For patients suspected of having raised CSF pressure, brain imaging with MRI and MR venogram is recommended as the initial study. Lumbar puncture should be performed when symptomatic to measure CSF pressure and assess response to CSF removal (20–30 mL). Elevated opening pressure and headache improvement post-CSF removal confirm diagnosis in absence of fundal changes.
keyPoints: MRI with MR venogram as the initial imaging study
Lumbar puncture when symptomatic; measure CSF pressure, remove 20–30 mL
Elevated opening pressure and headache improvement post-CSF removal confirm diagnosis
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for NDPH
items.content: Empirical treatment of NDPH is based on the headache phenotype. Initial management includes acetazolamide (250–500 mg bid), with weight loss if necessary. If ineffective, topiramate is recommended due to its multiple beneficial actions. Other treatments include tricyclic antidepressants like amitriptyline and anticonvulsants such as valproate and gabapentin. CGRP pathway blockers and phenelzine may also be useful in selected cases.
keyPoints: Initial treatment: acetazolamide (250–500 mg bid)
Topiramate if initial treatment is ineffective
Other treatments: tricyclic antidepressants, anticonvulsants, CGRP pathway blockers, phenelzine
Headache typically resolves within 3–5 years
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosing Primary vs. Secondary Headaches: A Key Task for Primary Care Physicians
items.content: Primary care physicians must differentiate between primary headaches, like migraines and tension-type headaches, and secondary headaches that may indicate more serious conditions. The focus should be on identifying worrisome causes or reassuring the patient when no specific diagnosis is made.
keyPoints: The majority of headache patients in primary care have primary headaches, with >90% presenting as migraine.
Investigations should aim to identify secondary headaches that are potentially serious.
Follow-up is crucial to assess treatment efficacy and progression of symptoms.
Referral to a specialist is recommended if diagnosis remains unclear or the patient does not respond to two or more standard therapies.
items.reference: Headache · PRIMARY CARE AND HEADACHE MANAGEMENT

items.title: When to Refer Headache Patients: Guidelines for Primary Care Physicians
items.content: Primary care physicians should refer patients who do not have a clear diagnosis, present with primary headache disorders other than migraine or tension-type headache, or are unresponsive to multiple standard treatments. The decision is influenced by the physician's experience and available secondary care options.
keyPoints: >90% of primary care headache presentations are migraines.
Referral is advised if diagnosis is unclear or if patients do not respond to two or more therapies for their headache type.
The threshold for referral depends on the physician’s expertise in headache management and secondary care availability.
items.reference: Headache · PRIMARY CARE AND HEADACHE MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which of the following structures are not pain-producing in the context of headache?
options: Proximal segments of large pial arteries
items.explanation: The ventricular ependyma, choroid plexus, pial veins, and much of the brain parenchyma are not pain-producing.

options: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
items.explanation: The International Headache Society has developed a classification system for headache disorders.

items.subtopic: Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE
items.question: In the context of primary headaches, what is the role of cranial autonomic symptoms?
options: They are causative events in headache disorders.
They reflect activation of cranial parasympathetic pathways.
They indicate vascular changes driving the headache.
They are unrelated to the trigeminovascular system.
items.explanation: Cranial autonomic symptoms reflect activation of cranial parasympathetic pathways and are secondary events in the headache cascade.
items.reference: Headache · Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE
items.question: Which statement best describes migraine according to the passage?
options: Migraine is primarily a vascular disorder.
Migraine treatment outcomes can be predicted by vascular effects.
Migraine is a brain disorder and not a 'vascular headache'.
items.explanation: Migraine is described as a brain disorder, not a 'vascular headache', and does not reliably manifest vascular changes.
items.reference: Headache · Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE

items.question: Which imaging modality is considered equally sensitive as an initial screening procedure for intracranial pathology in patients with new-onset severe headache?
items.explanation: Both CT and MRI are equally sensitive for initial screening of intracranial pathology.

items.question: What should be evaluated to identify comorbidity in patients with acute headache?
items.explanation: The psychological state is evaluated to identify comorbidity, not as a direct cause of the headache.

items.question: Which condition is a rare cause of isolated headache?
items.explanation: A brain tumor is a rare cause of isolated headache.

items.question: What symptom pattern suggests meningitis rather than migraine?
options: Severe headache that worsens with eye movement
Headache with vomiting preceding by weeks
items.explanation: Striking accentuation of pain with eye movement suggests meningitis.
items.reference: Headache · MENINGITIS | INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE | BRAIN TUMOR

items.question: What is highly characteristic of posterior fossa brain tumors?
options: Headache disturbing sleep in 10% of patients
Vomiting that precedes headache by weeks
Severe headache with stiff neck and fever
items.explanation: Vomiting that precedes the appearance of headache by weeks is highly characteristic of posterior fossa brain tumors.
items.reference: Headache · MENINGITIS | INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE | BRAIN TUMOR

items.question: Which symptom is most commonly associated with temporal arteritis and often appears in association with malaise and muscle aches?
items.explanation: Headache is the dominant symptom of temporal arteritis.
items.reference: Headache · TEMPORAL ARTERITIS

items.question: What is a major complication of untreated temporal arteritis?
items.explanation: Ischemic optic neuropathy induced by giant cell arteritis can lead to rapidly developing bilateral blindness.
items.reference: Headache · TEMPORAL ARTERITIS

items.question: What is the initial treatment recommended when clinical suspicion of temporal arteritis is high?
items.explanation: Immediate treatment with prednisone is recommended when temporal arteritis is suspected.
items.reference: Headache · TEMPORAL ARTERITIS

items.subtopic: GLAUCOMA | PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS | CHRONIC DAILY OR NEAR-DAILY HEADACHE | Chronic Daily Headache
items.question: What is the minimum frequency for headaches to be classified as chronic daily headache (CDH)?
items.explanation: CDH is defined by headaches occurring on 15 or more days per month.
items.reference: Headache · GLAUCOMA | PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS | CHRONIC DAILY OR NEAR-DAILY HEADACHE | Chronic Daily Headache

items.explanation: Aspirin is not listed as a preventive treatment for CDH in the passage.

items.explanation: The passage states that using a preventive when analgesics continue to be used regularly is a common cause.

items.question: Which condition must be excluded first when diagnosing new daily persistent headache due to its seriousness?
items.explanation: Subarachnoid hemorrhage is the most serious secondary cause of NDPH and must be excluded first.

items.question: What is a common initial treatment for low CSF volume headache?
items.explanation: Initial treatment for low CSF volume headache is bed rest, but IV caffeine can be very effective if pain persists.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 1)

items.question: What imaging study is the initial choice to identify a presumed source of CSF leak?
items.explanation: An MRI with gadolinium is the initial study of choice to identify a presumed source of CSF leak.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 1)

items.question: What symptom pattern suggests a diagnosis of low CSF volume headache?
options: Headache that worsens during the day and improves upon reclining
Severe headache with high blood pressure
items.explanation: Low CSF volume headache typically presents as a headache that worsens during the day and improves upon reclining.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a typical presentation of idiopathic intracranial hypertension associated with NDPH?
options: Headache that worsens throughout the day and improves with recumbency
Headache present on awakening, improving as the day goes on, and worsening with recumbency
Transient visual obscurations only during severe headaches
items.explanation: Patients typically have a headache that is present on awakening, improves as the day progresses, and worsens with recumbency.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 2)

items.question: What initial diagnostic test is most efficient for evaluating raised CSF pressure in patients suspected of having idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
items.explanation: An MRI, including an MR venogram, is the most efficient initial study for evaluating raised CSF pressure.

items.question: Which medication is recommended as first-line treatment for idiopathic intracranial hypertension associated with NDPH?
items.explanation: Initial treatment is typically with acetazolamide (250–500 mg bid).
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 2)

items.question: What should be considered in patients who do not respond to medical treatment for idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
options: Intracranial pressure monitoring and possibly shunting
items.explanation: Severely disabled patients who do not respond to medical treatment require intracranial pressure monitoring and may need shunting.
items.reference: Headache · NEW DAILY PERSISTENT HEADACHE (part 2)

items.question: Which type of headache disorder is most likely to be diagnosed when a patient presents with a complaint of headache in primary care?
items.explanation: More than 90% of patients presenting to primary care with headaches are diagnosed with migraine.
items.reference: Headache · PRIMARY CARE AND HEADACHE MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is the general approach for managing headache in the absence of warning signs?
options: Initiate treatment upon diagnosis and focus on identifying worrisome causes
Delay all treatments until further tests are conducted
items.explanation: The approach is to treat when a diagnosis is established and focus on identifying worrisome causes or gaining patient confidence.
items.reference: Headache · PRIMARY CARE AND HEADACHE MANAGEMENT

items.question: When should a primary care physician consider referring a headache patient to a specialist?
options: If the patient has a clear diagnosis of migraine
If the patient is responsive to standard therapies
If there's no clear diagnosis, or if unresponsive to two or more standard therapies
Only when the primary care physician lacks experience
items.explanation: Referral should occur when there's no clear diagnosis, a non-migraine/non-tension-type headache disorder is suspected, or lack of response to multiple therapies.
items.reference: Headache · PRIMARY CARE AND HEADACHE MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Primary headaches are caused by exogenous disorders.
items.explanation: Primary headaches are those in which headache and its associated features form the disorder itself, not caused by exogenous disorders.

items.explanation: Life-threatening headache is relatively uncommon, requiring vigilance to recognize and treat appropriately.
items.reference: Headache · Headache | GENERAL PRINCIPLES | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF HEADACHE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE
items.statement: A lumbar puncture is always required in the evaluation of new-onset severe headache.
items.explanation: A lumbar puncture is only required unless a benign etiology can be otherwise established.
items.reference: Headache · CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE
items.statement: The management of secondary headache involves treating the underlying condition.
items.explanation: Secondary headaches are managed by diagnosing and treating the primary cause.
items.reference: Headache · CLINICAL EVALUATION OF ACUTE, NEW-ONSET HEADACHE

items.statement: A ruptured aneurysm may present with headache alone.
items.explanation: A ruptured aneurysm can indeed present with headache as the sole symptom.

items.statement: The majority of patients presenting with severe headache have a serious underlying condition.
items.explanation: The vast majority of patients presenting with severe headache have a benign cause, usually migraine.
items.reference: Headache · MENINGITIS | INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE | BRAIN TUMOR

items.statement: The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is always elevated in patients with giant cell arteritis.
items.explanation: A normal ESR does not exclude the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis.

items.statement: Migraineurs often report improvement in their headaches with prednisone, which can complicate the interpretation of therapeutic response.
items.explanation: Prednisone may ameliorate migraines, thus complicating its use as a diagnostic tool for temporal arteritis.

items.statement: Post-LP headaches can be delayed for up to 12 days after the lumbar puncture.
items.explanation: Post-LP headache onset may indeed be delayed for up to 12 days.

items.statement: Spinal MRI with T2 weighting cannot reveal a CSF leak.

items.statement: Persistent Epstein-Barr virus infection is a confirmed cause of NDPH.
items.explanation: There is no convincing evidence that persistent Epstein-Barr virus infection plays a role in NDPH.

items.statement: Posttraumatic headache can develop after events such as viral meningitis or intracranial surgery.
items.explanation: Headache can develop following an injury to the head, infectious episodes like viral meningitis, or intracranial surgery.

items.statement: Follow-up care after initiating treatment for headaches is unnecessary if the patient initially responds well.
items.explanation: Follow-up care is essential to assess progress and identify whether worrisome headaches are progressing.

items.statement: The primary challenge for physicians in headache management is identifying the majority of primary headaches.
items.explanation: The challenge lies in distinguishing a few worrisome secondary headaches from the majority of less dangerous ones.

items.assertion: Migraine is best understood as a vascular headache.
items.reason: Vascular changes are not reliably present in migraine disorders.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because migraine is not considered a vascular headache; the reason is true.

items.assertion: Underlying recurrent headache disorders may be activated following certain surgical procedures.
